7 
Enhancing Supply Chain Efficiency through Predictive 
Analytics: A Systematic Approach to Forecast Unit of 
Activity 
Y.F. Lim1, V. Mirje1 
1 SLB 
 
 
Summary 
 
The supply chain of the oil and gas industry is known for its complexity and uncertainty. Lasker (2022) conducted 
a comprehensive study to examine the challenges of the industry’s supply chain and the potential of data analytics 
to improve its performance. Our own supply chain faces similar difficulties, particularly in predicting Unit of 
Activity (UoA) such as transaction volume, which currently relies on inconsistent and manual methods due to 
decentralized data and diverse measurement approaches. 
Existing approaches do not provide a systematic way of predicting UoA, and prone to inaccuracies and 
inefficiencies. To address these issues, this study proposed predictive analytics with a combination of statistical 
methods and machine learning models to forecast purchase order transaction volume. 
According to Lee et al. (2022), predictive analytics utilizes data, statistical techniques, and machine learning 
algorithms to predict future events and trends, ultimately improving organizational performance. The proposed 
methodology follows a defined process, including project definition, data collection, data analysis, statistical 
analysis, modelling, deployment, and model monitoring. 
By embracing predictive analytics, proposed approach offers a more efficient and reliable method for UoA 
prediction, contributing to optimal supply chain performance.

Introduction 
 
The supply chain within the oil and gas industry is renowned for its intricate nature and the multitude 
of operational activities it entails, all of which are accompanied by significant levels of uncertainty. 
Recognizing these complexities, Laskar (2022) undertook a comprehensive study to delve deeper into 
the challenges inherent in the industry's supply chain. The study's primary objective was to explore the 
potential of data analytics in addressing these challenges and enhancing overall supply chain 
performance. In line with the industry's complexities, our own supply chain faces similar hurdles. 
Specifically, the current process of predicting Unit of Activity (UoA) namely transaction volume relies 
on inconsistent and manual approaches. This inconsistency stems from the decentralized nature of data, 
which is derived from multiple tools and systems. Moreover, various subject matter experts employ 
diverse methods for UoA measurement and estimation, further exacerbating the challenge. 
 
The existing approaches utilized in UoA prediction fail to provide a systematic framework that ensures 
accuracy, scalability, and sustainability in the long run. Consequently, inefficiencies and inaccuracies 
persist within the system, hindering optimal supply chain performance. Recognizing these 
shortcomings, it is crucial to embrace an improved methodology that leverages advanced techniques 
and tools to streamline UoA prediction and enhance the overall supply chain process. 
 
To address the aforementioned issues, this study proposed employing predictive analytics with a 
combination of statistical approaches and machine learning models to predict purchase order transaction 
volumes, providing a systematic, auditable, transferable, scalable, and sustainable approach to UoA 
forecasting. According to Lee et al. (2022), predictive analytics leverages input data, statistical 
techniques, and machine learning algorithms to forecast the likelihood of specific events occurring and 
predict future trends or outcomes. Its ultimate goal is to enhance the performance of organizations by 
deriving insights into future trends or outcomes from existing data. The procedures involve define 
project, data collection, data analysis, statistical analysis, modelling, deployment and model monitoring 
(Lee et al., 2022).  
 
Unit of Activity (UoA) Analytics 
 
Unit of Activity (UoA) prediction is considered a regression problem because it involves estimating 
quantifiable measures which is continuous numerical values such as transaction volume (Seyedan & 
Mafakheri, 2020). Regression models are well-suited for UoA prediction as they can capture the 
complexity and variability of the data. In this study, the proposed approach utilizes future activities, 
such as rig count, type of rig, and activity rate, as drivers to predict UoA trends. This helps identify and 
plan around critical elements that impact efficiency and cost in supply chain operations. 
 
To ensure the temporal consistency of the data and mimic real-world scenarios accurately, the data is 
split into training and test sets by year instead of using a random ratio. Three years of historical data 
(2019-2021) are used to train the model, while a test set including more recent data (2022) is used for 
evaluating model performance. This approach allows the assessment of how well the model generalizes 
to future data. 
 
Before evaluating the model performance, Spearman correlation analysis is conducted to assess the 
correlation score between rig count and transaction volume. The analysis result showed high correlation 
score between rig count and transaction volume. This indicates a strong association between the 
variables, highlighting the reliability of rig count as an indicator for predicting transaction volume in 
the context of UoA prediction. 
 
The R2 score is utilized as an evaluation criterion to assess model performance. A higher R2 score 
suggests a larger proportion of variance in the UoA that can be explained by the predictor (rig count), 
indicating a stronger ability to accurately predict UoA values. The tested models in this study include

XGBoost, Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), Lasso (L1) regression, Ridge (L2) regression, Isolation 
Forest, K Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Random Forest. Among all the models, Random Forest 
demonstrates a high R2 score (see Figure 1). It outperforms other models by effectively capturing 
nonlinear relationships between input variables and the target variable. Random Forest is also robust to 
outliers and noisy data, making it reliable for UoA prediction. Additionally, it offers feature importance 
analysis and performs well with high-dimensional data, addressing challenges faced by other models.

Figure 1 R2 Score Comparison of Various Regression Models 
 
The prediction results of transaction volume using Random Forest model achieve an impressive 
accuracy rate of 89%. Accuracy is calculated by dividing the number of correctly predicted instances 
by the total number of instances. These predictions provide valuable insights into overall trends and 
fluctuations in activity levels, enabling informed decision-making and effective resource allocation 
across various hubs based on the estimated transaction volumes. This strategic approach to resource 
allocation and planning minimizes bottlenecks, maximizes efficiency, and enhances the overall 
performance of the supply chain. 
 
Framework Overview 
 
All in all, the organizational strategy is generated at highest level, incorporating inputs from various 
participants and supply chain activities through multiple support functions (see Figure 2). To enable 
predictive analytics, data from the supply chain environment is utilized. The process of predicting 
transaction volume through predictive analytics with machine learning begins by defining the Unit of 
Activity (UoA) and collecting data from multiple sources while adhering to proper business rules. Data 
analysis is then conducted to assess the quality of the data sources. Statistical analyses, including 
correlation analysis (Spearman), are performed to evaluate the relationships among different factors. 
After extensive evaluation of feature correlations and model performance, the random forest model is 
selected and implemented due to its ability to handle complex data and generate robust predictions. The 
prediction outputs are visualized using Power BI, which aids in result interpretation and supports 
strategic decision-making within the organization. The insights obtained through predictive analytics 
are valuable for performance measurement and continuous improvement, ultimately enhancing the 
overall efficiency of the supply chain.
